one_line_takeaway: Two lightweight retriever-only defenses—RAGPart (partition-and-mean-pool document fragments) and RAGMask (mask tokens that spuriously boost query similarity)—cut RAG corpus-poisoning attack success across four retrievers and four attacks while keeping benign utility, notably beating perplexity/paraphrase baselines that collapse on interpretable (non-gradient) poisons.
key_finding: Both RAGPart and RAGMask reduce attack success rate across all four attacks and both datasets while preserving benign utility, outperforming the paraphrase and perplexity baselines. The baselines only work against gradient-based attacks: paraphrasing and perplexity filtering both fail on interpretable attacks (Query-as-Poison, AdvRAGgen). This is concretely visible in the perplexity Table 1 — gradient attacks raise perplexity far above the clean baseline of 143 (e.g., ANCE on NQ: 5726 for HotFlip, 12021 for HotFlip spread-out), but the interpretable attacks produce perplexity at or BELOW clean (NQ Query-as-Poison = 119, AdvRAGgen = 74; FIQA = 100 and 53), so perplexity cannot separate them from benign documents. Between the two proposed defenses there is a trade-off: RAGPart gives slightly higher ASR reduction, while RAGMask better preserves utility (smaller SR drop) but is more computationally expensive per document. The theoretical analysis (Tables 2-5) shows RAGPart satisfies the robustness condition for many more (N,k) pairs than the naive baseline (which almost never holds, only at N>=11, k=3 for np=2); and a FLOPs example (R=1e9, D=1e6, N=5, k=3, ne=512) shows RAGPart cuts the dominant embedding cost ~6x vs naive concatenation (5e15 vs 3e16 FLOPs).
core_contribution: The paper proposes two complementary RETRIEVAL-STAGE defenses against corpus-poisoning attacks on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), both of which operate only on the retriever and require no change to the generation LLM. (1) RAGPart partitions each document into N fragments, embeds each fragment individually with the dense retriever, then mean-pools the embeddings over all (N choose k) size-k fragment combinations to form candidate document representations; multiple top-p sets are retrieved and aggregated (via majority vote). Because embeddings are averaged AFTER individual encoding, the influence of a few poisoned fragments is diluted. (2) RAGMask sanitizes the top alpha*p retrieved documents by masking spans of length m and measuring the similarity shift with the query: if masking a span does not drop similarity below the original score minus delta (i.e., v' + delta > v), the tokens are treated as adversarial and removed, then documents are re-ranked and the top-p returned. The paper also contributes (a) AdvRAGgen, an interpretable DPO-trained poison-paraphrase attack used to stress-test the defenses, and (b) a theoretical analysis showing RAGPart satisfies the robustness (majority-vote) condition for far more (N,k) settings than a 'naive' concatenate-then-embed baseline. NOTE: the compared generation-stage certified defense (Xiang et al. 2024, RobustRAG), DPA (Levine & Feizi 2021; Sun et al. 2022), RAP (Yang et al. 2021), and the poisoning attacks PoisonedRAG (Zou et al. 2024) / Zhong et al. 2023 are all cited prior work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Corpus poisoning of a RAG pipeline: an adversary injects maliciously crafted documents into the retrieval corpus so that a poisoned document is (i) retrieved for a target query (retrieval condition) and (ii) able to steer the LLM's generation (generation condition). The paper evaluates four attacker capabilities: white-box gradient attacks (HotFlip-style, and a 'HotFlip spread-out' variant that distributes adversarial tokens across the document), and black-box/interpretable attacks (Query-as-Poison, i.e., inserting the query text into the malicious document, and the paper's own AdvRAGgen paraphrase attack). Attacker's aim in the experiments is to make 3 randomly chosen irrelevant documents per query become retrievable. The defender controls only the retrieval stage and assumes retrievers are much smaller/cheaper than the generation LLM, so retrieval-stage operations are parallelizable.
limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that retrieval-stage defenses cannot stop poisons that are genuinely semantically similar to the query but factually wrong (their example: query 'what is the capital of France?' with poison 'The capital of France is Berlin'), because such documents are only identifiable as malicious at generation time, not by a retriever that encodes only semantic similarity. Additional reviewer-noted limitations: headline ASR/SR numbers are given only as averaged bar charts in the main text (exact per-retriever values are in the appendix, not extractable here); RAGPart's majority-vote aggregation loses the clean DPA robustness guarantee (np < (N-1)/2) unless p=1, which hurts utility; intersection-based aggregation gives stronger ASR reduction but is discarded because it drops utility too much; RAGPart cost grows with (N choose k); and evaluation is limited to two QA datasets and single-query-target poisoning.

datasets_used: FIQA (Financial Opinion Mining and QA), corpus of 57,638 documents
512 queries sampled; 3 irrelevant docs per query targeted for poisoning; >10 golden docs per query for benign utility
quantitative_results: Perplexity Table 1 (clean 'No Poison' baseline = 143 for all retrievers on both datasets)
NQ perplexity, ANCE retriever: HotFlip = 5726, HotFlip spread-out = 12021 (gradient attacks detectable)
NQ perplexity, Contriever: HotFlip = 989, HotFlip spread-out = 1827
Interpretable attacks evade perplexity: NQ Query-as-Poison = 119, AdvRAGgen = 74 (both below clean 143); FIQA Query-as-Poison = 100, AdvRAGgen = 53
FLOPs example (R=1e9, D=1e6, N=5, k=3, ne=512): naive baseline = 3e16 FLOPs vs RAGPart = ~5e15 FLOPs, ~6x reduction in dominant embedding cost
Theory Tables 2-5: for np=2 the naive baseline satisfies the majority-vote robustness condition only for N>=11 at k=3, whereas RAGPart satisfies it for many (N,k) pairs from N=5 upward
Multi-adversary robustness bound: total poisoned mixes must be < (na/(na+1))*(N choose k)
ASR/SR headline results reported as averages over the 4 retrievers, shown as bar charts (Figures 7-8); RAGPart gives slightly larger ASR drop, RAGMask gives smaller SR (utility) drop
baselines_compared: Naive concatenate-then-embed fragment-combination baseline (internal ablation vs RAGPart)
Referenced (not run as tables in main text) generation-stage certified defense of Xiang et al. 2024 as the impractical prior art being improved upon
